_what: The address registry — every valid ui:// address, its region, capabilities, the real feature it represents, and the code that powers it. Mirrors/extends the running app's UI_REGISTRY so a mockup element and its real component share ONE address. Add an addressable thing = add an entry here (extend-by-registration) + carry its data-ui-ref in the mockup; run parse.py to refresh element-map.json. Capabilities: pointable·spotlit·presentable·openable·driven (from contracts/ui_info.py).

ui://toolbar.howto: WHAT: the toolbar — the bridge's command strip across the top of the board (the Company title, the live presence light, then the act controls: run · wire · portal · delete · layers · fit · reload). WHAT YOU CAN DO: read the system's live state at a glance (running / thinking / awaiting) and reach every board-level action from one place. HOW TO CHANGE IT: the strip is the toolbar region (canvas/app/src/regions/Toolbar.tsx); adding or changing a control is a code change there, surfaced via the build-intent wire.

ui://toolbar/run.howto: WHAT: the Run control. WHAT YOU CAN DO: click it to recompute the current graph — every node whose inputs changed re-fires, cached nodes are skipped (the memo gate). HOW TO CHANGE IT: its behaviour lives in the toolbar region + the engine scheduler; changing what Run does is a code change to the run path, surfaced via the build-intent wire.

ui://toolbar/presence.howto: WHAT: the presence dial (the RHM mode selector). WHAT YOU CAN DO: pick a presence mode (focus, listening, walkthrough, …) — the mode changes which verbs the RHM is offered and how it speaks; off disables it. HOW TO CHANGE IT: modes are nodes (rhm_mode) + the MODES registry; add a mode by registering one.

ui://rail/palette.howto: WHAT: the palette — the rack of build-blocks (every node-type the system knows, grouped by kind: process · acts, content · holds). WHAT YOU CAN DO: click a block to drop that node on the board; select two and use ＋ wire to connect them. HOW TO CHANGE IT: the rack fills itself from the live registry (/object_info) — adding a node-type (nodes/<name>.py) makes a new block appear here automatically, no palette code.

ui://canvas.howto: WHAT: the canvas — the live board the whole composition sits on (pan, zoom, select; zoom in for more node detail — semantic zoom). WHAT YOU CAN DO: arrange and connect nodes spatially, select one to inspect it, select two to wire them; the board reflects live run-state. HOW TO CHANGE IT: the board is tldraw driven by NodeShape.tsx (the one generic node shape) + the App.tsx shell; structural canvas changes are external-agent code edits there.

ui://canvas/node.howto: WHAT: a node on the board — the one generic card every node-type renders as (its kind, status, output, address, and its input/output ports). WHAT YOU CAN DO: select it to inspect + edit its config, drag a port-nub to another node to wire it, watch its status colour change as the graph runs. HOW TO CHANGE IT: the card is the single generic NodeShape.tsx (NodeShapeUtil) — it draws every type from the registry, so a new node-type needs zero card code; what a node DOES lives in its nodes/<name>.py.

ui://inspector.howto: WHAT: the inspector — the detail panel for the node you have selected (its kind, status, node-id, address, an editable config form, and its output). WHAT YOU CAN DO: read a node's full state, edit its config inline (changes commit immediately), and act on its output (rerun · surface to inbox · build from it · open in workshop). HOW TO CHANGE IT: the panel is Inspector.tsx; the config form is generic from each type's config_schema (NodeConfigForm.tsx) — a new node-type gets a form with zero extra code.

ui://inspector/model-field.howto: WHAT: a config field on the selected node (e.g. the model dropdown). WHAT YOU CAN DO: pick or type a value; a model field offers the REAL models registered at the fabric endpoint (not invented names), and if a saved value isn't in the live list it's shown rather than silently dropped. HOW TO CHANGE IT: the field is generated from the node-type's config_schema (NodeConfigForm.tsx); the model list comes from the live registry (suite.py:available_models) — change the field by editing the type's schema.

ui://inspector/act.howto: WHAT: the ↻ force / rerun control in the inspector. WHAT YOU CAN DO: force this one node to recompute, bypassing the memo cache (run normally skips a node whose inputs haven't changed; force runs it anyway). HOW TO CHANGE IT: it drives the per-node run path (doRun([nodeId]) → the scheduler); the force-past-memo behaviour lives in runtime/scheduler.py.

ui://inbox.howto: WHAT: the inbox — your chief-of-staff triage deck: the decisions and approvals escalated to you, the decision→build lane, the held/deferred queue, and the resolved-for-you audit. WHAT YOU CAN DO: see what's awaiting you at a glance (by colour and shape, not by reading), drill into any item to decide it, or walk all awaiting items through the review organ. HOW TO CHANGE IT: the deck is Inbox.tsx; the lanes come from suite.py:inbox_lanes (live escalations / resolved / batched), test-pollution filtered at source.

ui://inbox/build-review.howto: WHAT: a build-result review card in the inbox. WHAT YOU CAN DO: read the result summary + the changed-files diff of a completed self-build, then approve (resolve) it — this records your verdict, it never re-triggers the build. HOW TO CHANGE IT: the review surfacing is the decision→implementation wire (guarded close + surface_review).

ui://inbox/build-intent.howto: WHAT: a build-intent card in the decision→build lane — a self-build the system has proposed, awaiting your approval. WHAT YOU CAN DO: read what it would build, see its declared scope + blast-radius (demonstrate-first), and approve it — your approve is consent OF THAT SCOPE, not a bare agree; it stays a live escalation until you resolve it. HOW TO CHANGE IT: minted by suite.py:surface_build_intent (intent='build' rides the payload), rendered by BuildIntentCard, resolved via /api/resolve (operator-only).

ui://inbox/context-bundle.howto: WHAT: the context bundle on a build-intent — the notebook the build will see at this locus (the comments, chats, history, and versions gathered at the pointed address). WHAT YOU CAN DO: see exactly what context the build's prompt will compose (a frozen snapshot taken at consent-time, so the build can't quietly resolve a different one), and click any item's address to navigate to it. HOW TO CHANGE IT: it renders payload.context, resolved by R2's gather/score/cap at mint-time (suite.py); the view is ContextBundle.tsx.

ui://inbox/context-pin.howto: WHAT: the pin control on a context item. WHAT YOU CAN DO: pin an item to HOLD it in the build's bounded context window — pinned items survive the recency/proximity decay that would otherwise let them fall out; click again to unpin. HOW TO CHANGE IT: it writes a pin overlay via /api/pin (suite.py:pin) that affects FUTURE gathers; this frozen snapshot reflects the change optimistically.

ui://inbox/coa.howto: WHAT: a decision card in an action lane (the ⚠ items awaiting you). WHAT YOU CAN DO: click it to compile a course-of-action — the system translates the raw technical decision into a Commander-altitude value choice (what it means, 2-3 options with trade-offs, and a recommendation); the raw payload stays drillable. HOW TO CHANGE IT: the compile is suite.py:coa (the decision-compiler); you decide on value, never the raw code fork.

ui://inbox/target.howto: WHAT: the ↳ go to thing link on a surfaced item. WHAT YOU CAN DO: click it to jump the view to the thing the item is about — it navigates to and spotlights that element in context (only shown when the item carries a target). HOW TO CHANGE IT: it composes resolveUiTarget (the click-to-thing keystone) off the item's payload.ui_target; the surfaced output is suite.py:surface_output.

ui://chat.howto: WHAT: the right-hand-man — the Company's chat about itself, the one coherent voice that answers from live system state. WHAT YOU CAN DO: ask it about the system (it answers from real state and says so when it can't see something), speak via the mic, configure its model + persona, and approve any action it proposes. HOW TO CHANGE IT: the surface is RhmChat.tsx; the brain is suite.py:chat — your turns are gold (they train the twin), its turns are working-grade inference.

ui://chat/input.howto: WHAT: the chat input box. WHAT YOU CAN DO: type a question about the system and press Enter (or the → send) to ask the right-hand-man; if you've pointed at an element, your next message is about that element (the indicated locus). HOW TO CHANGE IT: it sends to suite.py:chat; the message composes with the indicated address + the live context resolved at that locus.

ui://activity.howto: WHAT: the activity feed — the bridge's live-state read (the current graph, how many nodes resolved, what's awaiting you) plus the captured event trace. WHAT YOU CAN DO: see the run-state at a glance and scan the instrument trace of what the system has done (each event token-coloured by kind). HOW TO CHANGE IT: the head reads suite.py:now (live state); the feed is the captured trajectory — it reflects, it doesn't drive.

ui://walkthrough.howto: WHAT: the walkthrough card — the visible review organ, shown while a session is active. It runs in two shapes: a REVIEW walk (give a verdict on each pending item) or a guided TOUR (show me how — narrate + spotlight each part of the interface, no verdicts). WHAT YOU CAN DO: step through the session, hear/read each item framed in plain language, and (in a review) record a verdict. HOW TO CHANGE IT: the card is Walkthrough.tsx; the session is suite.py:present_current; verdicts go through /api/resolve.

ui://walkthrough/verdict.howto: WHAT: the verdict row in a review walk (approve · reject · comment · skip · decide). WHAT YOU CAN DO: give your verdict on the current item — approve it, reject with a reason, comment (capture a note, stays pending), skip (still needs you later), or let the system decide deterministically by consequence; one click = one verdict (the controls lock while a request is in flight). HOW TO CHANGE IT: each verdict goes through suite.py:resolve_surfaced via /api/resolve (operator-only); the build loop reads the verdicts on its next pass.

ui://workshop.howto: WHAT: the Workshop — where the operator composes and edits the system itself. WHAT YOU CAN DO: build panels, author extensions, and review self-changes. HOW TO CHANGE IT: the workshop region + the self-modification path (panel/extend verbs, build-gated, git-reversible).

ui://tabbar.howto: WHAT: the tabbar — the mobile navigation bar (the responsive chrome that lets you switch surfaces on a phone, where the side rails are hidden). WHAT YOU CAN DO: move between the board and the panels on a small screen. HOW TO CHANGE IT: it's the .tabbar in app.css + the App.tsx shell; it appears at narrow breakpoints — its layout is responsive chrome, not a backend feature.

ui://models.howto: WHAT: the models registry — the real models available to the system. WHAT YOU CAN DO: see which models actually exist at the fabric endpoint (so the brain picks from what's real, never an invented name); the list re-probes so a model that comes up later appears without a restart. HOW TO CHANGE IT: it reads suite.py:available_models (the live endpoint, fail-loud to the configured brain if it's down); add a model in fabric/config.py.

ui://twin.howto: WHAT: the twin's training signal — the gold turns that teach the Company's learned model of you. WHAT YOU CAN DO: see the operator-sourced gold turns that train the twin (only your own words count — never the twin's own output, even if it was laundered back as a 'user' turn). NOTE: the learned twin is corpus-pending; this surfaces the training signal it learns FROM. HOW TO CHANGE IT: it reads suite.py:training_signal (gold + source='operator' + an echo-guard); every chat turn you write is graded gold and feeds it.

ui://canvas/portal-window.howto: WHAT: a portal — a live window onto another address (one artefact, shown in many places at once, never copied). WHAT YOU CAN DO: see the current value at the address it points to, live — change the source and the portal reflects it, because it is resolved by reference at view-time, not computed. HOW TO CHANGE IT: it's the portal node-type (nodes/portal.py, RESOLVE='reference'); its target is its config.ref — edit that in the inspector to re-point the window.

ui://canvas/wire-request.howto: WHAT: the request-a-change door — the operator's entry to changing the Company through the interface. It appears when you point at a UI element. WHAT YOU CAN DO: describe the change you want to that element; it mints a build-intent (a PLAN) at that address, shows you the reach (the blast-radius of what the change could touch), and lets you approve it. Approving records your verdict — it does NOT self-modify: the wire is inert (plan-mode) by default until deliberately armed. HOW TO CHANGE IT: the door is WireRequest.tsx; minting calls /api/intent-at (suite.py:surface_intent_at) and approval calls /api/resolve — the dispatch happens off this face, posture-gated and exactly-once.

ui://workshop/self-changes.howto: WHAT: the self-changes audit + the ⟲ revert control. WHAT YOU CAN DO: see the last change the system made to itself, and revert it — a bounded, recoverable git-revert (the self-modification path is always git-reversible). HOW TO CHANGE IT: revert calls suite.py:revert_self_change; every self-apply is a git commit, so any change can be undone.

ui://activity/replay.howto: WHAT: replay — the whole captured path, oldest-first. WHAT YOU CAN DO: see the full trajectory the system has recorded (the same path that trains the twin), in order. HOW TO CHANGE IT: it reads suite.py:replay (the recent events, reversed to oldest-first); it's a read of the event log.

ui://toolbar/wire.howto: WHAT: the ＋ wire control. WHAT YOU CAN DO: select exactly two nodes on the board (left then right) and click it to connect them — it wires the left node's first output to the right node's first input; if the types have no compatible ports it tells you, never wires blindly. HOW TO CHANGE IT: the connect gesture is wireSelected in the controller (useAppController.ts); the wire itself is type-checked and owned by the backend (/api/connect).

ui://toolbar/portal.howto: WHAT: The portal control on the board's command strip. WHAT YOU CAN DO: Open the portal to access the system's deep configuration and extension layers. HOW TO CHANGE IT: It lives in the toolbar region (Toolbar.tsx); changing its behavior is a code change there, surfaced via the build-intent wire.

ui://toolbar/delete.howto: WHAT: the 🗑 delete control. WHAT YOU CAN DO: select node(s) and click it to remove them from the graph — it always confirms first (naming the nodes), so a single click can never irreversibly delete. HOW TO CHANGE IT: deleteSelected in the controller (useAppController.ts); the confirm guard is preserved by design — removing it would make delete a one-click destructive path.

ui://toolbar/layers.howto: WHAT: the ◐ layers control. WHAT YOU CAN DO: click to cycle the board's focus through three views — all · origin only (operator-authored nodes, system-derived ones dimmed) · system only (the reverse). It's a viewing lens, it changes nothing in the graph. HOW TO CHANGE IT: cycleLayers in the controller (useAppController.ts) toggles the dimming classes on the body.

ui://toolbar/fit.howto: WHAT: the ⊡ fit control. WHAT YOU CAN DO: click to zoom the board so the whole graph fits in view, padded so no node hides under the side panels or the toolbar (on a phone, where the rails are hidden, it pads zero automatically). HOW TO CHANGE IT: fitGraph in the controller (useAppController.ts); the padding is measured live from the rendered chrome, not hardcoded.

ui://toolbar/reload.howto: WHAT: the reload control. WHAT YOU CAN DO: click to re-read the graph from the backend — it refetches the nodes, edges, and live status and repaints the board, without recomputing anything (that's run). HOW TO CHANGE IT: reload in the controller (useAppController.ts); it reloads the graph, polls state, and re-runs the watch-and-react probe.

ui://toolbar/point.howto: WHAT: the ◎ point toggle in the toolbar. WHAT YOU CAN DO: turn point mode on or off — when it's ON, tapping any element of the interface marks that element as the focus of your next message, so you can ask 'what is this?' about exactly what you tapped; when it's OFF, taps behave as normal clicks. WHEN YOU'D REACH FOR IT: turn it on when you want to point at parts of the surface and talk about them, off when you just want to operate the buttons normally. HOW IT WORKS: it flips the deliberate indicate gate (useAppController.ts:toggleIndicateMode); while on, a tapped element ships its ui:// address to the chat as the indicated locus.

ui://chat/send.howto: WHAT: the → send control. WHAT YOU CAN DO: send the typed message to the right-hand-man (disabled while it's thinking, so one ask is one turn). HOW TO CHANGE IT: it calls sendChat → suite.py:chat in the controller.

ui://chat/mic.howto: WHAT: the 🎙 mic control — push-to-talk. WHAT YOU CAN DO: click to record your voice as the message; in listening mode the right-hand-man speaks its reply back. HOW TO CHANGE IT: it drives the voice capture path (the voice/ stack + recordToggle); speech-to-text and the spoken reply are the voice circuit.

ui://chat/minimize.howto: WHAT: the ▁ / ▢ minimize toggle in the chat's header. WHAT YOU CAN DO: collapse the chat down to just its header bar so you can see the canvas behind it — handy on a phone where the chat otherwise fills the screen — then click again to expand it back. WHEN YOU'D REACH FOR IT: when the chat is in the way of the graph you're looking at. HOW IT WORKS: it's a view-only toggle on the chat panel (the `.rhm.min` collapse) — it hides nothing, changes no config, and the restore button stays visible in the header.

ui://chat/new-conversation.howto: WHAT: the + new button above the chat. WHAT YOU CAN DO: start a fresh conversation with the right-hand-man — it clears the visible chat and opens a new thread, so a new topic doesn't pile onto the last one. WHEN YOU'D REACH FOR IT: when you want a clean slate without losing the old conversation (the previous thread is still there to reopen). HOW IT WORKS: it mints a new thread (suite.py:new_conversation) and makes it the current one; the old thread is kept and listed in the reopen picker beside it.

ui://chat/threads.howto: WHAT: the reopen-a-conversation dropdown beside + new. WHAT YOU CAN DO: pick one of your past conversations to reopen it — its history loads back into the chat so you can pick up where you left off. WHEN YOU'D REACH FOR IT: when you want to return to an earlier thread rather than start fresh. HOW IT WORKS: choosing a thread loads its saved history (suite.py:load_conversation) and makes it the current conversation; the list is the real saved threads (empty until you've had more than one).

ui://chat/record.howto: WHAT: the ● record / ■ stop-recording button in the chat's voice settings. WHAT YOU CAN DO: record this conversation as a session — each turn is saved so you can debrief it afterwards, and it feeds the model-of-you (the twin). WHEN YOU'D REACH FOR IT: when you want to keep a conversation for later review, not just chat-and-forget. HOW IT WORKS: starting it mints a trial session and saves each spoken turn (suite.py:trial_record_turn); stopping it ends the session — which you can then walk back through with debrief.

ui://chat/debrief.howto: WHAT: the debrief button in the chat's voice settings. WHAT YOU CAN DO: walk back through the conversations you recorded — each recorded session is surfaced for review with its real transcript, and you step through them the same way as a guided walkthrough. WHEN YOU'D REACH FOR IT: after recording one or more conversations, when you want to review what was said and give your verdict. HOW IT WORKS: it gathers your recorded sessions and starts a debrief over them (suite.py:start_debrief), reusing the walkthrough organ; the sessions appear in the inbox / walk to review.

ui://inspector/workshop.howto: WHAT: the open workshop control. WHAT YOU CAN DO: take this node into the Workshop — the surface for composing and editing the system itself (panels, extensions, self-changes). HOW TO CHANGE IT: it opens the workshop region for the selected node; the workshop is driven by the self-modification verbs.

ui://inspector/surface.howto: WHAT: the → inbox control. WHAT YOU CAN DO: route this node's output to the decision surface — it becomes a decision card in the inbox you (or the review organ) can drill into and act on. HOW TO CHANGE IT: surfaceOutput in the controller → suite.py:surface_review puts the result on the inbox; the surfacing is part of the review-not-free path.

ui://inspector/build.howto: WHAT: the ⊕ build from output control. WHAT YOU CAN DO: start a new node from this output — it prefills the grow panel's spec with the result so you can name it, refine it, and dispatch a build. HOW TO CHANGE IT: buildFromOutput in the controller seeds the grow→propose→approve chain (suite.py:apply_node); the build still goes through approval, never self-applies.

ui://inspector/history.howto: WHAT: the history view at an element — everything that has happened AT a pointed address. WHAT YOU CAN DO: when you point at a ui:// element, see its full trajectory grouped by kind (comments · chats · runs · resolves), scan the shape of what happened, and drill into a group. HOW TO CHANGE IT: it reads GET /api/address-history (suite.py:address_view); it only reads — the runtime owns the trajectory.

ui://inspector/versions.code: canvas/app/src/regions/Versions.tsx (L6 versions at an address) / runtime/suite.py:ref_versions / store/fs_store.py:ref_history
ui://inspector/versions.howto: WHAT: the versions view — the trail of values an address has HELD over time (newest first). WHAT YOU CAN DO: when a node is selected, see every value its output address has held, with a preview and content hash each; the current value is marked, the prior bytes survive (write-once). HOW TO CHANGE IT: it reads GET /api/ref-versions (suite.py:ref_versions → store ref_history); versions accrue wherever a set_ref wrote, so a portal shows the trail of the address it points at.

ui://inspector/freshness.howto: WHAT: the freshness badge on the selected node (fresh ✓ / stale ⚠ / always re-derived / unknown). WHAT YOU CAN DO: see whether the node's cached result still matches its current inputs — stale means rerun to refresh; it's a derived verdict (recompile + input-hash compare), and an unevaluable node reads 'unknown — <reason>', never a false 'fresh'. HOW TO CHANGE IT: it's derived on demand via GET /api/stale-at (suite.py:stale_at_address); the memo/staleness logic lives in the engine scheduler.

ui://grow.howto: WHAT: the grow panel — where you teach the system a new node + review what it proposes. WHAT YOU CAN DO: name a node and say what it should do, dispatch a build, then approve or reject the proposal the brain comes back with; you also see the live node-types and can revert the last self-change. HOW TO CHANGE IT: the panel is Grow.tsx; the build goes through propose→approve (suite.py:apply_node) — the brain never self-applies, you approve.

ui://grow/dispatch.howto: WHAT: the dispatch build → control. WHAT YOU CAN DO: after naming a node and describing what it should do, click it to send the build — the system drafts the node and comes back with a proposal for your verdict (it does not go live until you approve). HOW TO CHANGE IT: it dispatches the build (suite.py:apply_node) into the propose→approve chain; a build error surfaces fail-loud here.

ui://grow/approve.howto: WHAT: the ✓ approve & apply control on a proposal. WHAT YOU CAN DO: read the brain's proposal (the plain-language draft, optionally drilling to the raw code) and approve it — this is operator-initiated and applies the change; the brain never self-applies. HOW TO CHANGE IT: it goes through resolve + apply (runtime/governance.py:resolve); operator-only approval is preserved by design.

ui://grow/reject.howto: WHAT: the ✕ reject control on a proposal. WHAT YOU CAN DO: reject the proposal and (optionally) say why — your reason is captured into the trajectory, so the rejection teaches, not just stops. HOW TO CHANGE IT: it records a reject verdict with the reason (suite.py:resolve_surfaced); the reason-capture is the preserved reject path.

ui://walkthrough/voice.howto: WHAT: the voice / text toggle on the walkthrough. WHAT YOU CAN DO: choose whether each step is spoken aloud (voice-first) or shown as text only. HOW TO CHANGE IT: it sets voiceOn in the controller; voice-first speaks each step via the voice/ stack.

ui://walkthrough/next.howto: WHAT: the next → control on the walkthrough. WHAT YOU CAN DO: advance to the next step (disabled while a request is pending, so a second click can't desync the cursor). HOW TO CHANGE IT: it calls suite.py:next (the review/guide cursor advance).

ui://walkthrough/reason.howto: WHAT: the reason box on a review step. WHAT YOU CAN DO: type why — captured into the verdict (required for reject and comment), so your decision carries its rationale into the trajectory. HOW TO CHANGE IT: the reason rides the verdict to suite.py:resolve_surfaced.

ui://walkthrough/show-again.howto: WHAT: the ↪ show again control on a step. WHAT YOU CAN DO: re-spotlight the element this step concerns — it moves the view back to that thing and highlights it. HOW TO CHANGE IT: it re-runs resolveUiTarget on the step's ui_target; the per-step target is stamped by suite.py (_registry_ui_target).

ui://inbox/walk.howto: WHAT: the ▷ walk these control on the inbox. WHAT YOU CAN DO: send all the awaiting items through the review organ as one guided walk — each item is framed in plain language and you give a verdict (approve · reject · comment · skip · decide) step by step. HOW TO CHANGE IT: it starts a review session (suite.py:present_current); the walkthrough card is the visible organ.

ui://toolbar/guide.howto: WHAT: the ? guide control — the operator entry to a SYSTEM-INITIATED guided tour ("show me how"). WHAT YOU CAN DO: click it to step through the interface's addressed elements one at a time — each part is spotlit on the board and narrated from its own corpus how-to, no verdicts (it's a tour, not a review). HOW TO CHANGE IT: it calls start_guide (suite.py) which compiles a guided sequence over the registered ui:// addresses + drives the walkthrough organ; the tour's contents come from the corpus how-tos, so authoring a how-to enriches what the tour says with zero tour code.

ui://toolbar/teach.howto: WHAT: the ⚙ teach-me-to-self-modify control — the BOOTSTRAP tour that teaches the one move that unlocks everything else: how to change the Company from inside it. WHAT YOU CAN DO: click it to walk the request-a-change flow (point at an element → ask for a change → it surfaces as a build-intent → you approve), with the real wire-door, inbox lane, and approve control spotlit and narrated in turn. HOW TO CHANGE IT: it calls start_guide('request-a-change') (suite.py) — the SAME guided-sequence machinery as ? guide, on the request-a-change topic; its narration composes the teach script WITH the per-element corpus how-tos.

ui://inspector/help.howto: WHAT: the address-help panel — "what can I do here?" for the element you've pointed at, written at your altitude. WHAT YOU CAN DO: read the plain-language WHAT and how-to-use of the indicated element up top, then drill down (it's collapsed by default) into the mechanism — the code files it powers and the blast-radius of changing it. It shows nothing until you indicate a ui:// element. HOW TO CHANGE IT: it reads GET /api/address-help (suite.py:address_help), which composes the three legs (what-this-is · how-to-use · how-to-change) from the corpus; the panel only renders the bundle — it never composes a parallel one (registry-is-truth).

ui://settings.howto: WHAT: the consolidated Settings control-room — the one designed place the system's configuration lives (the brain & persona, presence modes, voice, model roles, and the composition knobs), opened from the ⚙ settings gear. WHAT YOU CAN DO: read the live config of every section at a glance and change what's changeable in one place (the model, persona, presence mode, auto-detect, voice ear/engine/path, role bindings); read-only/unavailable settings are shown honestly, never as working. HOW TO CHANGE IT: the surface is Settings.tsx; every write goes through the existing config path (suite.py:set_rhm_config) — it is reuse-only over the real config, not a new store.

ui://settings/close.howto: WHAT: the ✕ close control in the Settings masthead. WHAT YOU CAN DO: close the Settings control-room and return to the canvas — clicking the dimmed area around the panel does the same thing. WHEN YOU'D REACH FOR IT: when you're done changing config and want the surface out of the way; any change you made is already saved (each section writes as you change it), so closing loses nothing. HOW IT WORKS: it just dismisses the modal (useAppController.ts:setSettingsOpen(false)) — it is view-only, it does not save or discard, because the settings sections write live.

ui://settings/brain.howto: WHAT: the Brain & Persona section of Settings — the conscious model the right-hand-man thinks with, plus the switchable cast of personas. WHAT YOU CAN DO: pick the brain's model + base-url (empty model = the default), and switch the active persona (a switch cold-loads that persona's voice, evicting the previous). HOW TO CHANGE IT: model writes via set_rhm_config; persona switches via the persona path — the model + persona lists come from the live registry, never invented.

ui://settings/modes.howto: WHAT: the Modes section of Settings — the presence type-registry (each mode's directive, how it resolves context, its sub-types and consent) plus the auto-detect toggle. WHAT YOU CAN DO: switch the active presence mode (takes live), and set whether the system auto-detects the mode (off / suggest / auto) — the auto-detect toggle is live-settable. HOW TO CHANGE IT: the mode switch is changeMode; the auto-detect toggle writes MODE_AUTODETECT through set_rhm_config (validated off/suggest/auto, persisted, re-resolved so autodetect_mode honours it).

ui://settings/voice.howto: WHAT: the Voice section of Settings — voice on/off, the listening style (push-to-talk vs auto-listen), the ear (STT), the speaking engine (TTS), and the voice path (the pipeline). WHAT YOU CAN DO: turn spoken replies on or off (voice_enabled); choose HOW the mic finishes a spoken turn — 🎙 push-to-talk (tap to start, tap to stop) or 👂 auto-listen hands-free (it ends the turn when it judges you've finished a thought, not on a dumb timer); choose the STT provider and a TTS-engine override (or clear it to use the persona default); and pick the voice path. An engine that isn't up or a path that's unwired shows as UNAVAILABLE, never as working. HOW TO CHANGE IT: voice on/off + stt + tts_engine + voice_path write via set_rhm_config; the listening style writes the voice_input_mode slot (useAppController.ts:setVoiceInputMode → set_rhm_config, fail-loud on an unknown mode, takes effect on your next mic press); the engine list reflects what's actually up (s2s renders unavailable by design — no fiction).

ui://settings/roles.howto: WHAT: the Model Roles section of Settings — the model-FUNCTION registry (e.g. the fast judge), where each role binds to a model. WHAT YOU CAN DO: bind a model to a role (an unbound role is shown as awaiting); binding one role never wipes a sibling (the backend deep-merges the roles map). HOW TO CHANGE IT: it writes roles via set_rhm_config (the deep-merge preserves the other bindings); the model choices come from the live registry.

ui://settings/composition.howto: WHAT: the Composition section of Settings — the context-ranking knobs (X17) that tune how the system gathers and scores context. WHAT YOU CAN DO: read the live knob values; this section is READ-ONLY (the knobs are env-tuned, with no live setter) — surfaced honestly so you see the real values without a control that doesn't exist. HOW TO CHANGE IT: the knobs resolve from the composition config (env-tuned, X17); a future rung could expose live setters — until then they are read-only by design (no fiction).

ui://cognition.howto: WHAT: the live Cognition view — the ambient render of Concurrent Cognition, a glanceable shape of how the system is thinking right now (the Pulse at altitude 0; the River of role tributaries + node-states at altitude 1). WHAT YOU CAN DO: read the live turn's shape at a glance — how many roles fired, how much each contributed (the worn-channel width), whether any role failed (a loud notch), calm when idle; click to drop into the deeper River/Nodes altitude. It reflects-never-owns — it mirrors the cognition.* lifecycle the backend emits as a staged turn fires; it never drives cognition. HOW TO CHANGE IT: the view is the CognitionView region; what it renders FROM is the cognition_info projection (roles -> River tributaries, node_states -> status tokens, registry-driven); changing the view is a code change there, surfaced via the build-intent wire; changing what cognition DOES is role/rule authoring (operator-gated).

ui://cognition/pulse.howto: WHAT: the Pulse — altitude 0 of the Cognition view, the always-on ambient shape (dilation = roles fired + chars injected; a loud notch if any role FAILED; calm when idle). WHAT YOU CAN DO: read the current turn's cognition shape by sight without opening anything; click it to open the deeper River altitude (the per-role tributaries). It is folded purely from the live cognition.* lifecycle — reflects-never-owns. HOW TO CHANGE IT: the Pulse is part of the CognitionView region; its shape is derived live from the folded turn frame (foldCognition); changing how it reads is a code change there, surfaced via the build-intent wire.

ui://cognition/river.howto: WHAT: the River — altitude 1 of the Cognition view, the per-role tributaries of a staged turn (each role a channel whose width is its contribution — the worn-channel/budget=attention shape; a failed role reads as a loud silted gap). This is the STATIC fallback address; a LIVE turn carries its own run:// turn address (turn.address) as the data-ui-ref, so the River addresses the actual turn when one is flowing. WHAT YOU CAN DO: read which roles fired and how much each shaped the reply, by sight. reflects-never-owns — it mirrors the cognition.* lifecycle; it never drives cognition. HOW TO CHANGE IT: the River is part of the CognitionView region; what it renders FROM is the live turn frame (foldCognition) + the cognition_info projection; changing it is a code change there, surfaced via the build-intent wire.

HOW TO CHANGE IT: the surface is composed from named primitives (components/StudioKit.tsx); its look is filled by Claude Design via the --studio-* token-slots; its seams are declared in components/StudioSeams.ts.

ui://studio/rail.howto: WHAT: the gallery rail — the corpus of reviewable mockups, grouped, bound from the live corpus index (registry-is-truth, never a hardcoded list). HOW TO CHANGE IT: the corpus is the mockup files on disk (design/mockups/) joined to curated meta in runtime/bridge.py:_CORPUS_META.

It NEVER executes — comment attaches, request mints a plan. HOW TO CHANGE IT: comment posts via /api/annotate (annotate), request posts via /api/intent-at (surface_intent_at); the look is filled via --studio-composer-* token-slots.

ui://studio/rhm.howto: WHAT: the right-hand-man panel — the REAL conversational organ (same brain, persona, threads as the canvas), bound to the indicated surface's locus. HOW TO CHANGE IT: it reuses the chat organ (/api/chat with focus={selected:[ui://…]}); the look is filled via --studio-rhm-* token-slots.

ui://studio/rhm/help.howto: WHAT: the locus help strip — what the pointed surface IS and what a change here would touch (the composed address-help). HOW TO CHANGE IT: it posts to /api/mockup-generate which calls the configurable generate engine (runtime/generate_mockup.py); the behaviour (template, model, plan-vs-apply, routing) is declared data in design/_system/generate-config.json — edit the config, no code change.

WHAT YOU CAN DO: Ask it about the system state, listen to its voice, record sessions, and configure its model and persona. HOW TO CHANGE IT: The surface is RhmChat.tsx; the brain is suite.py:chat — your turns train the twin, and its working-grade inference drives the system.

ui://chat/utterance.howto: WHAT: A summary statement of the memo-gate change awaiting review. WHAT YOU CAN DO: Read the summary to understand the change, then approve, reject, comment, or skip it as part of the review process. HOW TO CHANGE IT: The surface is the Walkthrough region (RhmChat.tsx); the content generation is driven by the system's state analysis (suite.py:chat).

ui://chat/walk.howto: WHAT: The 'Walkthrough' view on the chat interface, where the system highlights a specific item to guide the operator's attention. WHAT YOU CAN DO: Approve, reject, decide, comment, or skip the current highlighted item; you can also speak to the system to push a voice command. HOW TO CHANGE IT: The surface is RhmChat.tsx; the session logic is suite.py:chat (specifically the WALK-session wire for guiding attention).

ui://inbox/audit.howto: WHAT: The audit footer on the forensic dashboard. WHAT YOU CAN DO: View the complete chronological path of events for a decision, read the raw activity log, and review the specific causal chain that authorized the final outcome. HOW TO CHANGE IT: The footer is the visible organ of the dashboard region; the log tracing logic lives in the runtime suite (audit tracing).

ui://inbox/build-review/foot.howto: WHAT: The status footer on the build review card. WHAT YOU CAN DO: Read the live state of the build (live, closed, implemented) and the event stream connection status. HOW TO CHANGE IT: It lives in the build-review region (runtime/suite.py:review_verdicts); changing the status text is a code change there.

ui://inbox/build-review/lifecycle-states.howto: WHAT: The legend explaining the lifecycle outcomes of a dispatched build. WHAT YOU CAN DO: Read the rules governing how a build either closes successfully or returns as a specific failure state for review. HOW TO CHANGE IT: The legend text is the decision→implementation wire (guarded close + surface_review); changing it requires a code update in the review verdicts region.

ui://inbox/build-review/verdict.howto: WHAT: A verdict marker on the build-review card indicating the item's current state. WHAT YOU CAN DO: Read the status ('held', 'deferred', 're-offered') to understand the item's lifecycle stage and required action. HOW TO CHANGE IT: The state is determined by the review verdict logic in suite.py:review_verdicts; changing the visible label requires updating that verdict logic.

ui://inbox/decision.howto: WHAT: The decision card in the inbox's action lane, presenting a pre-triaged value-choice. WHAT YOU CAN DO: Drill into the item to see its ground truth or open the build review; the system translates the raw technical decision into a Commander-altitude value choice (what it means, 2-3 options with trade-offs, and a recommendation). HOW TO CHANGE IT: The card is the visible organ in Inbox.tsx; the compilation logic is suite.py:coa (the decision-compiler); you decide on value, never the raw code fork.

ui://inbox/idea.howto: WHAT: A triage note in the inbox indicating a mid-flow item that has resurfaced for review. WHAT YOU CAN DO: Read the status of the resurfaced item and the recommended action (turn into a build-intent or defer). HOW TO CHANGE IT: The note is surfaced in Inbox.tsx; the underlying state and recommendation logic live in suite.py:inbox_lanes.

ui://inspector/status.howto: WHAT: The live status bar on the Node Inspector panel. WHAT YOU CAN DO: Read the node's current state (live, running, inputs cached, resolution count, and event stream connection) at a glance. HOW TO CHANGE IT: It lives in the Inspector.tsx region; the status text is driven by the node's runtime state (likely via a state wire or status query).

ui://rail/palette/node-kinds.howto: WHAT: The 'Node palette' — the rack of build-blocks displaying every known node-type (grouped by kind: process, acts, content, holds). WHAT YOU CAN DO: Click a block to drop that node on the board; select two and use the wire tool to connect them. HOW TO CHANGE IT: The rack fills itself from the live registry; adding a node-type (nodes/<name>.py) makes a new block appear here automatically, no palette code change required.

ui://settings/endpoint.howto: WHAT: The endpoint configuration field within the Living Instrument settings panel. WHAT YOU CAN DO: Read the provider base URL for the current endpoint and update it if the setting is changeable. HOW TO CHANGE IT: The surface is Settings.tsx; the value is managed via the existing config path (suite.py:set_rhm_config) under the RHM-config feature.

ui://tabbar/canvas.howto: WHAT: The 'Canvas' navigation button in the mobile tabbar. WHAT YOU CAN DO: Tap this to switch the view to the live 'running chain' map, where you can zoom and interact with the data flow visualization. HOW TO CHANGE IT: It is the 'Canvas' link in the .tabbar region (canvas/app/src/App.tsx); changing its label or icon is a code change there.

ui://tabbar/inbox.howto: WHAT: The Inbox entry point in the mobile navigation bar. WHAT YOU CAN DO: Switch the view to the Inbox surface where decisions and approvals are managed. HOW TO CHANGE IT: It lives in the .tabbar region (App.tsx); changing the label or icon is a code change there.

ui://toolbar/force.howto: WHAT: The force control on the toolbar. WHAT YOU CAN DO: Click this to immediately trigger a forced system action, overriding normal state checks. HOW TO CHANGE IT: It lives in the toolbar region (canvas/app/src/regions/Toolbar.tsx); changing its behavior is a code change there.

ui://toolbar/presence/resp.howto: WHAT: The text-only response switch on the presence dial. WHAT YOU CAN DO: Click this to force the RHM to reply only in concise text, ignoring visual or complex modes; click again to restore the previous posture. HOW TO CHANGE IT: It lives in the presence dial region (runtime/suite.py:set_mode); changing the text-only behavior is a code change there.

ui://twin/addresses.json.howto: WHAT: The address ledger for the Twin system, displaying the current node configuration. WHAT YOU CAN DO: View the current addresses and their state within the Twin's operational context. HOW TO CHANGE IT: It reads from the twin's configuration region (runtime/suite.py:training_signal context); changes require updating the twin's address mapping.
